title: Setup & Prerequisites
text: For the live code on the Foundations page you need nothing but a browser. For the hands-on exercises you need R and RStudio. Allow about 20 minutes.

title: Setup & Prerequisites
text:
1. Software

R ≥ 4.3: https://cran.rstudio.com/
RStudio (latest): https://posit.co/downloads/

title: Setup & Prerequisites
text:
2. R packages
In RStudio, run once:
install.packages(c("tidyverse", "here", "janitor", "skimr", "pacman", "MASS"))
tidyverse gives you dplyr, ggplot2, readr and friends. here builds reliable file paths. janitor::clean_names() tidies column names. skimr::skim() summarises a dataset. MASS supplies the birthwt dataset used in the exercises.

title: Setup & Prerequisites
text:
3. Project
Open DMgtAsia_DataScience_IntroR.Rproj in RStudio. Working with an RStudio Project means here() paths resolve from the project root, so your code runs the same on any machine.

title: Setup & Prerequisites
text:
4. Pre-flight check
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
cat("R", as.character(getRversion()), "ready\n")
If that runs without error, you are ready. Open Session-I.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
EDA is the critical first step.
EDA is a state of mind.
EDA is exploring your ideas.
EDA has no strict rules.
EDA helps understand your data.
EDA is an iterative cycle.
EDA is a creative process.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
EDA is the critical first step.
EDA is a state of mind.
EDA is exploring your ideas.
EDA has no strict rules.
EDA helps understand your data.
EDA is an iterative cycle.
EDA is a creative process.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
Definition of EDA

“detective work – numerical detective work – or counting detective work – or graphical detective work”


Tukey, 1977 Page 1, Exploratory Data Analysis

title: Working with Data in R
text:
How to do EDA?

The easiest way to do EDA is to use questions as tools to guide your investigation.
EDA is an important part of any data analysis, even if the questions are known already.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
Asking the right questions
Key to asking quality questions is to generate a large quantity of questions.
It is difficult to ask revealing questions at the start of the analysis.
But, each new question will expose a new aspect and increase your chance of making a discovery.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
What Questions to ask?

What type of variation occurs within your variables?
What type of covariation occurs between your variables?
Whether your data meets your expectations or not.
Whether the quality of your data is robust or not.

title: Working with Data in R
section: Steps of a data analysis project
text:
Steps of a data analysis project



Preparing Tidy Data

Data Cleaning
Data Wrangling

Data Exploration

Transformation
Visualization

Statistical Analysis
Prepare Results
Draw Inferences
Report Findings

title: Working with Data in R
text:
Data Wrangling in R

dplyr Package


The dplyr is a powerful R-package to manipulate, clean and summarize unstructured data.
In short, it makes data exploration and data manipulation easy and fast in R.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
Verbs of the dplyr

There are many verbs in dplyr that are useful.

title: Working with Data in R
text: Using the pipe operator (|> or %>%)

title: Working with Data in R
text:
Let us load some data
The cells on this page run live in your browser. This first one loads the WHO TB data and runs on its own (the first run takes a few seconds while dplyr loads).

title: Working with Data in R
section: Take a Quick Look at the data
text:
Take a Quick Look at the data
glimpse() shows every column down the page. Press Run.

title: Working with Data in R
text: Check the first few rows

title: Working with Data in R
section: Check the dimensions and the column names
text: Check the dimensions and the column names

title: Working with Data in R
section: Unique countries in the bottom 50 rows
text:
Unique countries in the bottom 50 rows
With the pipe, the steps read left to right: take tb, keep the last 50 rows, list the distinct countries.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
distinct() and count()
distinct() returns the distinct values of a column. count() returns each distinct value and how many times it appears. Try both.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
arrange()
arrange() sorts by a column. Use desc() (or a leading -) to sort descending. Edit and re-run.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
Logical Operators in R



To satisfy all of several conditions, use the “and” operator, &.
The “or” operator | returns rows that meet any of the conditions.

title: Working with Data in R
text: filter(): rows from 2015 on

title: Working with Data in R
text: filter(): all entries for India

title: Working with Data in R
text: Filter by year and country

title: Working with Data in R
text:
The %in% operator
To filter a categorical variable for several levels at once, use %in%. indian_subcont was defined in the first cell.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
summarize()
summarize() collapses many values to one. n() counts rows; n_distinct() counts distinct values.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
group_by() and summarize()
The power of summarize() shows when you group first. Summaries are then computed per group.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
mutate() and if_else()
mutate() creates or changes a column. Here we flag the Indian subcontinent.

title: Working with Data in R
text: Group by the new variable

title: Working with Data in R
text:
case_when()
Alternative to if_else() for more than two options.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
if_else() vs case_when()
Use if_else() for two options. For more options, nested if_else() becomes hard to read, so case_when() is clearer.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
join()

Real projects combine several sources of data.
The mutating joins combine data frames by matching rows on a key.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
More on join()

left_join(x, y) keeps all rows of x, adding matching columns from y.
right_join(x, y) keeps all rows of y.
full_join(x, y) keeps all rows from both.
inner_join(x, y) keeps only rows present in both.

title: Working with Data in R
text: Visual representation of the left_join()

title: Working with Data in R
text:
pivot()
We often reshape data between long and wide format with the pivot family of functions.

title: Working with Data in R
text:
Other Useful Functions

drop_na() removes rows with missing values in a chosen column.
select() returns only the columns you name (the column counterpart of filter()).

title: Working with Data in R
text:
More Resources for dplyr

The Data Transformation cheatsheet summarises the dplyr verbs on a single page.
The RStudio IDE cheatsheet is a handy reference for the editor itself.
Review the Tibbles and Transformations chapters of the free R for Data Science book.

EDA is the critical first step.
EDA is a state of mind.

EDA has no strict rules.

EDA is a creative process.

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
Public health runs on data: surveys, surveillance, registers, trials.
Data science is the workflow that turns that data into decisions: import, tidy, transform, visualise, model, communicate.
We do it in a way others can check and repeat.



The live R cells on this page run in your browser. Nothing to install.

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
Public health runs on data: surveys, surveillance, registers, trials.
Data science is the workflow that turns that data into decisions: import, tidy, transform, visualise, model, communicate.
We do it in a way others can check and repeat.



The live R cells on this page run in your browser. Nothing to install.

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
Why R and RStudio

R is a language built by and for statisticians and epidemiologists.
It is free, open, and has a package for almost every method in public health.
RStudio is the workbench that makes R comfortable to use.
Everything is scripted, so an analysis is a document you can re-run, not a set of clicks.

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
The RStudio tour

Source (top left): your scripts and Quarto documents.
Console (bottom left): R runs here.
Environment (top right): the objects you have created.
Files / Plots / Help (bottom right): outputs and documentation.
Projects keep one analysis self-contained; with here(), file paths just work.

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
Objects and assignment (live)
Press Run. <- stores a value in a named object.
The keyboard shortcut for <- is Alt/Option and minus.

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
Vectors (live)
A vector holds several values of the same type. c() combines them.
Try changing a number and running again.

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
Data types

numeric 3.4, counts and measurements.
character "female", text in quotes.
logical TRUE / FALSE.
factor a category with fixed levels (for example sex, district).

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
Functions

A function takes inputs and returns an output: mean(weights).
You name the function, then pass arguments in brackets.
summary() describes a whole vector at once.

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
Packages

A package is a bundle of functions. Install once, load each session.
install.packages("tidyverse") downloads it (done on your machine).
library(tidyverse) loads it for use.

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
Recap

Data science is a workflow: import, tidy, transform, visualise, communicate, reproducibly.
R is the language, RStudio is the workbench, Projects plus here() keep paths sane.
Objects (<-), vectors (c()), data types, and functions are the building blocks.
Packages add capability: install once, library() each session.
Next: best practices, then working with real data.

title: Foundations of R & RStudio
text:
Data science for public health

Public health runs on data: surveys, surveillance, registers, trials.
Data science is the workflow that turns that data into decisions: import, tidy, transform, visualise, model, communicate.
We do it in a way others can check and repeat.



The live R cells on this page run in your browser. Nothing to install.

title: About
text: Introduction to R and RStudio is the data science foundations module of the DMgtAsia course, and the precursor to the Spatial Epidemiology module. It draws on data science trainings delivered across India and is packaged here as a self-paced or workshop module with live code and hands-on exercises.

title: About
text: Introduction to R and RStudio is the data science foundations module of the DMgtAsia course, and the precursor to the Spatial Epidemiology module. It draws on data science trainings delivered across India and is packaged here as a self-paced or workshop module with live code and hands-on exercises.

title: About
text:
Author

Dr. Arun Mitra, Assistant Professor, Department of Community & Family Medicine, All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS), Bibinagar, Hyderabad, Telangana

title: About
text:
Source & licence
Source repository: https://github.com/drarunmitra/DMgtAsia_DataScience_IntroR
Teaching materials are released under CC-BY-SA; code under MIT, unless noted otherwise.

title: About
text:
Acknowledgements
Built on R, the tidyverse, quarto-live (webR), and Quarto.

title: Introduction to R and RStudio
text:
Welcome
This is the data science foundations module of the DMgtAsia course, and the precursor to the Spatial Epidemiology module. It introduces R and RStudio for public health work, from first principles to a reproducible analysis.
No prior R experience is assumed. The first thing you run will work.

title: Introduction to R and RStudio
section: What you will be able to do
text:
What you will be able to do

Explain why R and a reproducible workflow suit public health.
Find your way around RStudio: panes, Projects, and here().
Use R basics: objects, vectors, data types, functions, and packages.
Read a dataset, inspect it, and wrangle it with the tidyverse.
Follow good data science practice: reproducibility, project structure, and coding style.

title: Introduction to R and RStudio
text:
Sessions








Session
Theme
Focus




Session-I
Foundations
R, RStudio, the data science workflow, R basics


Session-II
Best Practices in Data Science
reproducibility, tool choice, literate programming, structure, style


Session-III
Working with Data
exploratory data analysis and the dplyr verbs


Session-IV
Exercises
hands-on practice on a real dataset



Start with Setup to prepare your machine, then open Session-I.

title: Introduction to R and RStudio
text:
Live code
The Foundations session runs R in your browser (webR), so you can edit and run code on the page with nothing installed. For the full hands-on work you use R and RStudio on your own machine.

title: Introduction to R and RStudio
text:
Acknowledgements
Developed at AIIMS Bibinagar and the Achutha Menon Centre for Health Science Studies, SCTIMST. Built on R, the tidyverse, and Quarto.

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Genesis

Digital Platforms for Disease Surveillance and Control: Best Practices from Asia (DMgtAsia)
Bridge the gap between public health and technology
Post COVID-19: Need for data-driven decision-making at scale
Limited training capacity in LMICs in this area
Develop standardized, reproducible best practices that can be adapted globally

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Why Best Practices Matter


Provide Guidance to navigate the complexities of data science
Plethora of information on Data Science available, without clear direction
Handholding in your learning journey, not learning in isolation
Steep Learning Curve: structured pathways reduce frustration

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Why Best Practices Matter (cont.)


Inevitable use of AI: a Human in the Loop approach
Set you apart from the crowd: credibility through proven methods
Consistency among trainers, to maintain quality

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
What are these Best Practices?


Based on a Scientific Approach

Grounded in epidemiological principles and data science methodology
Validated in the real-world and through peer-reviewed literature




Tried & Tested for over a decade of working in this Area

SCTIMST: Public Health Technologies Training Modules
Resource Faculty: Years of collective experience in teaching

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
What are these Best Practices?


Walk the Talk

We actively use these practices in our day-to-day teaching & research
We make conscious decisions to use these best practices in our own work
Self critique and Support one another




Worked for over 300+ learners from over 75+ institutions

Medical professionals, informaticians, data scientists, healthcare workers
Recognised a Best Practice in Public Health Teaching (IAPSMCON-2024)
Multiple Verticals

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
What are these Best Practices?


State of the Art tools

R: Developed by and for statisticians and epidemiologists
tidyverse: Industry-standard for reproducible workflows
Quarto: A Language Agnostic Dynamic Authoring Framework




Industry Standard

Aligns with international best practices
Recognized by WHO, CDC, UNICEF, and global health agencies
Facilitates collaboration with other research teams

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Core Philosophies


Data Science is a Tool for Social Good, Not an End in Itself




Data for Better Lives




3D Commission Recommendations




FAIR Data Principles




The Public Health Lens
Bridging Disciplines

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text: What is Reproducibility

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text: FAIR Principles and Reproducibility

text: FAIR and CARE

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text: FAIR Principles and Reproducibility

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text: Reproducible Workflows

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text: The Reproducibility Spectrum

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Why R (and not other tools)?


Born from S language, specifically designed for statistical analysis
The global epidemiology and biostatistics community uses R
Hundreds of packages for health research
Open-source: you can see how calculations are done
Windows, Mac, Linux: runs even on older computers
Thousands of tutorials, Stack Overflow answers

title: Best Practices in Data Science
section: R vs. Alternatives for Health Research
text:
R vs. Alternatives for Health Research








Tool
Strength
Limitation




Excel
Accessible, intuitive
Prone to errors, not reproducible


SPSS/Stata
User-friendly GUI
Expensive, less transparent, limited Customization Options


Tableau/Jamovi
Less Expensive than others
Limited Analysis Options


Python
General-purpose, flexible
Steeper learning curve for epidemiologists


R
Purpose-built for stats, reproducible, free
Slightly steeper initial learning curve

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
The R Ecosystem


R Programming Language: The Engine!
RStudio IDE: The Dashboard!
Quarto/R Markdown: Combine narrative text + code + output (reproducible documents)
tidyverse: Collection of R packages designed for modern data workflows

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
What is Literate Programming?

Code + Narrative + Output = Single Document


Start with a DATA DICTIONARY

The Concept (Donald Knuth, 1984)

Programs should be written for people to read, not just for machines to execute
Mix explanation with code
Generate analysis reports that are reproducible and transparent

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
In Practice: Quarto/R Markdown
Write it once. The document weaves narrative, code, and output together:



attack_rates.qmd
# Age-Stratified Attack Rates

Attack rates by age group for the Sholayur Acute Gastroenteritis outbreak.

## Data Preparation

```{r}
age_data <- read_csv(here("data", "family_members.csv")) |>
  mutate(age_group = factor(age_group,
    levels = c("0-4", "5-17", "18-64", "65+")))
```

## Results

```{r}
age_data |>
  group_by(age_group) |>
  summarise(
    n_symptomatic = sum(symptomatic, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_total       = n(),
    attack_rate   = n_symptomatic / n_total * 100
  )
```

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Project Organization
A well-organized project is a well-organized mind.
Standard Directory Structure
my_analysis/
├── README.md              # Project overview
├── data/
│   ├── raw/              # Never modify
│   │   └── gastroenteritis_sholayur.csv
│   ├── processed/        # Cleaned, validated data
│   │   └── gastroenteritis_clean.csv
│   └── data_dictionary.md
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_data_cleaning.R
│   ├── 02_descriptive_analysis.R
│   └── 03_inferential_stats.R
├── results/
│   ├── tables/
│   ├── figures/
│   └── outputs/
├── reports/
│   ├── outbreak_report.qmd
│   └── outbreak_report.html
├── docs/
│   ├── protocol_v3.pdf
│   └── iec_approval.pdf
└── .gitignore

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text: Naming Conventions

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Naming Conventions

Scripts:

stroke_data_exploration.R, dengue_data_visualization.R, instead of scrpit1, script2
Can consider 01_, 02_ … for indicating sequential execution

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Naming Conventions (cont.)


Variables:

age_group not ageGrp or AgGr (consistency matters)
Do not start variable names with numbers
Consider adding prefix/suffix to indicate variable type (state_fct, age_group_chr)
dem_q1, dem_q2, dem_q3, health_financing_q1, health_financing_q2
Add variable labels if necessary using the labelled package

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Naming Conventions

Files:

outbreak_analysis_2025_11_07.R (date in YYYY_MM_DD format)

Dates:

Always ISO 8601 (YYYY-MM-DD) in code for consistency across regions
Use _date suffix for indicating dates

Versions:

Use version for better management outbreak_analysis_2025_11_07_v2.R instead of outbreak_analysis_2025_11_07_final_final.R

title: Best Practices in Data Science
Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Example: Good vs. Bad

BadGood


d<-read.csv('file.csv')
d$ag<-cut(d$a,c(0,5,18,65,150))
d$ar<-ifelse(d$d==1,1,0)*100/d$t


# This script creates age_group variable from age column

# Load Packages
pacman::p_load(tidyverse, here)

# Load Data
age_data <- read_csv(here("data", "age_sholayur.csv"))

# Create age groups with meaningful labels
age_data <- age_data |>
  mutate(
    age_group = case_when(age < 5 ~  "0-4",
                          age < 18 ~  "5-17",
                          age < 65 ~  "18-64",
                          age >= 65 ~  "65+")
        ) |>
  mutate(
    age_group = factor(age_group, 
                        levels = c("0-4", "5-17", "18-64", "65+"))) |>
  )

title: Best Practices in Data Science
section: AI is Now Part of the Workflow
text:
AI is Now Part of the Workflow
The use of AI is inevitable. Using it well is a choice.

It writes code, drafts text, and explains errors in seconds
But it is fluent and confident even when it is wrong
What you get back depends on the quality of what you ask for
Stay the Human in the Loop: AI assists, you decide



The prompt, not the model, decides whether the output is any good.

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Four prompting strategies








Strategy
What it is
Best for




Zero-shot
Just ask, no examples
Descriptive stats


Explicit
Numbered steps incl. checks
Workflows that mustn’t skip steps


Chain-of-thought
“Think step by step”
Auditing the reasoning


Hybrid
Steps + reasoning + format
Inferential — the default




Rule of thumb: descriptive → zero-shot; inferential → hybrid.

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Example: Vague vs. Briefed

VagueBriefed


Analyse this data and run a t-test.
Skips the assumptions, guesses the design, picks a test for you.


You are a biostatistician.
<context> two-arm trial, 20 patients per arm,
outcome is systolic blood pressure </context>
<data> ... </data>

1. Test for normality and equal variance.
2. Choose the correct test and justify it.
3. Report a table: statistic, df, p-value, 95% CI.
4. Interpret in plain language.

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Iterate, Do Not Perfect

The first answer is the average answer; the second is usually better
Refine in small steps rather than crafting one perfect prompt
Ask for alternatives and for the reasoning, not just the result
Resist sycophancy: ask the model to critique, not to confirm

“Grade this analysis against these criteria” beats “this looks fine, right?”

title: Best Practices in Data Science
section: Prompting is Part of Your Methods
text:
Prompting is Part of Your Methods
The same discipline as Reproducibility (#2) and Literate Programming (#4).

Save the prompts that worked, inside the project (prompts/)
Record the model, its version, and the date
Set a low temperature for analysis you must reproduce
Validate every AI-generated number in R before it enters a report
Never paste identifiable patient data into a cloud LLM



Problem formulation beats perfect wording. Think first, prompt second.

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Benefits of Sharing and Acknowledging

No single person has all the expertise needed in public health data science.
Opportunity to learn from peers
Improves your credibility & citations
Opens avenues for collaboration
Self Marketing!

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Digital Platforms for Disease Surveillance and Control: Best Practices from Asia (DMgtAsia)
Bridge the gap between public health and technology
Post COVID-19: Need for data-driven decision-making at scale
Limited training capacity in LMICs in this area
Develop standardized, reproducible best practices that can be adapted globally






Provide Guidance to navigate the complexities of data science
Plethora of information on Data Science available, without clear direction
Handholding in your learning journey, not learning in isolation
Steep Learning Curve: structured pathways reduce frustration







Inevitable use of AI: a Human in the Loop approach
Set you apart from the crowd: credibility through proven methods
Consistency among trainers, to maintain quality






Based on a Scientific Approach

Grounded in epidemiological principles and data science methodology
Validated in the real-world and through peer-reviewed literature




Tried & Tested for over a decade of working in this Area

SCTIMST: Public Health Technologies Training Modules
Resource Faculty: Years of collective experience in teaching







Walk the Talk

We actively use these practices in our day-to-day teaching & research
We make conscious decisions to use these best practices in our own work
Self critique and Support one another




Worked for over 300+ learners from over 75+ institutions

Medical professionals, informaticians, data scientists, healthcare workers
Recognised a Best Practice in Public Health Teaching (IAPSMCON-2024)
Multiple Verticals







State of the Art tools

R: Developed by and for statisticians and epidemiologists
tidyverse: Industry-standard for reproducible workflows
Quarto: A Language Agnostic Dynamic Authoring Framework




Industry Standard

Aligns with international best practices
Recognized by WHO, CDC, UNICEF, and global health agencies
Facilitates collaboration with other research teams

text: The Reproducibility Spectrum

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Why R (and not other tools)?



Born from S language, specifically designed for statistical analysis
The global epidemiology and biostatistics community uses R
Hundreds of packages for health research
Open-source: you can see how calculations are done
Windows, Mac, Linux: runs even on older computers
Thousands of tutorials, Stack Overflow answers

title: Best Practices in Data Science
section: R vs. Alternatives for Health Research
text:
R vs. Alternatives for Health Research









Tool
Strength
Limitation




Excel
Accessible, intuitive
Prone to errors, not reproducible


SPSS/Stata
User-friendly GUI
Expensive, less transparent, limited Customization Options


Tableau/Jamovi
Less Expensive than others
Limited Analysis Options


Python
General-purpose, flexible
Steeper learning curve for epidemiologists


R
Purpose-built for stats, reproducible, free
Slightly steeper initial learning curve

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
The R Ecosystem


R Programming Language: The Engine!
RStudio IDE: The Dashboard!
Quarto/R Markdown: Combine narrative text + code + output (reproducible documents)
tidyverse: Collection of R packages designed for modern data workflows

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
What is Literate Programming?

Code + Narrative + Output = Single Document


Start with a DATA DICTIONARY


The Concept (Donald Knuth, 1984)

Programs should be written for people to read, not just for machines to execute
Mix explanation with code
Generate analysis reports that are reproducible and transparent

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
In Practice: Quarto/R Markdown
Write it once. The document weaves narrative, code, and output together:



attack_rates.qmd
# Age-Stratified Attack Rates

Attack rates by age group for the Sholayur Acute Gastroenteritis outbreak.

## Data Preparation

```{r}
age_data <- read_csv(here("data", "family_members.csv")) |>
  mutate(age_group = factor(age_group,
    levels = c("0-4", "5-17", "18-64", "65+")))
```

## Results

```{r}
age_data |>
  group_by(age_group) |>
  summarise(
    n_symptomatic = sum(symptomatic, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_total       = n(),
    attack_rate   = n_symptomatic / n_total * 100
  )
```

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Project Organization
A well-organized project is a well-organized mind.

Standard Directory Structure
my_analysis/
├── README.md              # Project overview
├── data/
│   ├── raw/              # Never modify
│   │   └── gastroenteritis_sholayur.csv
│   ├── processed/        # Cleaned, validated data
│   │   └── gastroenteritis_clean.csv
│   └── data_dictionary.md
├── scripts/
│   ├── 01_data_cleaning.R
│   ├── 02_descriptive_analysis.R
│   └── 03_inferential_stats.R
├── results/
│   ├── tables/
│   ├── figures/
│   └── outputs/
├── reports/
│   ├── outbreak_report.qmd
│   └── outbreak_report.html
├── docs/
│   ├── protocol_v3.pdf
│   └── iec_approval.pdf
└── .gitignore

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text: Naming Conventions

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Naming Conventions

Scripts:

stroke_data_exploration.R, dengue_data_visualization.R, instead of scrpit1, script2
Can consider 01_, 02_ … for indicating sequential execution

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Naming Conventions (cont.)


Variables:

age_group not ageGrp or AgGr (consistency matters)
Do not start variable names with numbers
Consider adding prefix/suffix to indicate variable type (state_fct, age_group_chr)
dem_q1, dem_q2, dem_q3, health_financing_q1, health_financing_q2
Add variable labels if necessary using the labelled package

title: Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Naming Conventions

Files:

outbreak_analysis_2025_11_07.R (date in YYYY_MM_DD format)

Dates:

Always ISO 8601 (YYYY-MM-DD) in code for consistency across regions
Use _date suffix for indicating dates

Versions:

Use version for better management outbreak_analysis_2025_11_07_v2.R instead of outbreak_analysis_2025_11_07_final_final.R

title: Best Practices in Data Science
Best Practices in Data Science
text:
Example: Good vs. Bad

BadGood


d<-read.csv('file.csv')
d$ag<-cut(d$a,c(0,5,18,65,150))
d$ar<-ifelse(d$d==1,1,0)*100/d$t


# This script creates age_group variable from age column

# Load Packages
pacman::p_load(tidyverse, here)

# Load Data
age_data <- read_csv(here("data", "age_sholayur.csv"))

# Create age groups with meaningful labels
age_data <- age_data |>
  mutate(
    age_group = case_when(age < 5 ~  "0-4",
                          age < 18 ~  "5-17",
                          age < 65 ~  "18-64",
                          age >= 65 ~  "65+")
        ) |>
  mutate(
    age_group = factor(age_group, 
                        levels = c("0-4", "5-17", "18-64", "65+"))) |>
  )

title: Hands-on Exercises
text: Practise the workflow from Sessions I to III on a real dataset, in RStudio. Work through the tasks; each has a collapsible solution. The full scripts are in the repo: exercises/d1_low_birth_weight_demo.R and exercises/d1_low_birth_weight_solutions.R.

title: Hands-on Exercises
text:
The dataset
We use birthwt from the MASS package: 189 births from a study of risk factors for low birth weight. Key variables: low (birth weight under 2.5 kg, yes/no), age (mother’s age), lwt (mother’s weight), smoke (smoking during pregnancy), bwt (birth weight in grams).

title: Hands-on Exercises
text:
1. Load packages and data
if (!requireNamespace("pacman", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("pacman")
pacman::p_load(MASS, tidyverse, skimr, janitor, here)

lbw <- as_tibble(MASS::birthwt)

title: Hands-on Exercises
text:
2. Tidy the column names






Note



The birthwt names are terse. Make them tidy and consistent.








TipSolution





lbw <- lbw |> janitor::clean_names()
names(lbw)

title: Hands-on Exercises
text:
3. Take a first look






Note



How many rows and columns? What types are the variables? Any missing values?








TipSolution





glimpse(lbw)
skimr::skim(lbw)

title: Hands-on Exercises
text:
4. Recode for readability






Note



Turn low and smoke from 0/1 into meaningful labels.








TipSolution





lbw <- lbw |>
  mutate(
    low_birth_weight = factor(low, levels = c(0, 1),
                              labels = c("Normal", "Low")),
    smoking          = factor(smoke, levels = c(0, 1),
                              labels = c("Non-smoker", "Smoker"))
  )

title: Hands-on Exercises
text:
5. Summarise






Note



What is the mean birth weight by smoking status? How many low-birth-weight babies by smoking status?








TipSolution





lbw |>
  group_by(smoking) |>
  summarise(
    n         = n(),
    mean_bwt  = mean(bwt),
    pct_low   = mean(low == 1) * 100
  )

title: Hands-on Exercises
text:
6. Visualise






Note



Plot the distribution of birth weight, and compare it by smoking status.








TipSolution





ggplot(lbw, aes(bwt)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20) +
  labs(x = "Birth weight (g)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(lbw, aes(smoking, bwt)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Birth weight (g)") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on Exercises
text:
Stretch tasks

Does mean birth weight differ by mother’s smoking after grouping by race?
Make a clean summary table with gtsummary::tbl_summary().
Save your cleaned data to data/processed/lbw_clean.csv with readr::write_csv() and here().

When you are done, you are ready for the Spatial Epidemiology module.
